speaker: interviewer
text: This is the second part with Mr. Yehudah Bakon. The date today is the 31st of December, 1996. I am Yael Ravon. And this is going to be tape number 9.

speaker: interviewer
text: We finished our last tape talking about your life in Auschwitz, in the different blocks that you were in. And at the end where you spoke-- at the end of our last meeting, you spoke about staying in block number 13, the strafkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: But still there are some things that I would like to discuss with you which maybe are more or less general. And then we'll go back to the story. Things that sort of popped out-- up. Like, for instance, I would like to ask you, how was it about dreams? Did you have dreams during your stay, for instance in-- let's go back to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you dream?

speaker: interviewee
text: I dreamt that I am back in my place-- I mean, where we were. And I am back, just when I left before, after my bar mitzvah. And I just started my collection of post stamps and books which I got for bar mitzvah and all the newspaper-- the children newspaper I collected. And I got it back. And, of course, the dream was to be back in Mahrisch Ostrau-- in Ostrava, today. And, later on, in Auschwitz, it was so far away, to be at home in Ostrava, that all that I remember I could dream about was that I back in Theresienstadt. After Birkenau, Theresienstadt was a dream, in comparison with Auschwitz-Birkenau

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, you could dream-- you had dreams at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had dreams, This dream I remember very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it all? Could you sleep well at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- at least we children-- we could sleep quite well, because we didn't had so much sleep. We had to get up very early for the appell. And during the day we had to walk or go around and do whatever we had to do. And the drills in the morning appell, and the drills in the evening appell. So people were exhausted. And also we children were very happy to go-- to be able to go to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you slept on [NON-ENGLISH] together with other boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with the other boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you arrived in Auschwitz, at the first, did you have to write any postcards to your relatives, or to friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at once. But we were forced to write postcards with a date about a couple of weeks ahead, and especially before they separated us. Meaning the old transport--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, date-- different date?

speaker: interviewee
text: A different date.

speaker: interviewer
text: More advanced date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or more before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of course, advanced. And we had to send these postcards to friends, to Theresienstadt. And I remember I organized a group of us, I mean children, and we send it to the same address from where we came, from Theresienstadt, to this children home. And all of us finished this postcard-- Apropos, we were only allowed to write 30 words, and only the first and second person, like I and-- and you, and that's that. But we finished it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which language did you write?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German. But we finished it in--

speaker: interviewer
text: It had to be in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: It had to have be in German, as far as I remember. At least we wrote it in German. And wrote, and with this, dear moti. And moti means my death in Hebrew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and this word every one of us knew-- I will finish. And we know now, because we asked our friends after the war, if they got these postcards, and they said yes. And they wondered, what does it mean, moti, that all of our boys wrote the same? But they couldn't imagine that it really means the deaths. I also know about it that some people didn't care anymore and wrote explicitly that we are going to die in gas. They will kill us in gas-- hoping that the censorship--

speaker: interviewer
text: Using the word gas.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I used the word moti, my death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the same group of children I organized for this purpose. But even these postcards came through. But nobody could imagine that the people will be killed in gas. By the way, even in Auschwitz elderly people, mainly, didn't believe it. I mean, at least from our transport. Said no, they are transport at night, and the people who are going on selections. And only you children have this, or the other people, have this imagination that they go through the chimney they are killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even people who were in Auschwitz didn't believe that they will be-- that there is gas going on, just next around the corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all, but many didn't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some, of course. Some.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I learned that the will to live and not to believe was actually was going on was very strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back for a second, to the postcards. Now, when the Germans gave you the postcard, did they tell you what to write? Or you could write anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they restricted us to 30 words--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and first and second person. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you could write anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you don't have such a choice. You could only write, I am fine. How are you? And it was obvious we are not allowed to write--

speaker: interviewer
text: I am suffering.

speaker: interviewee
text: --about sufferings, and so on. And mainly to hint that we would like food or so on-- parcels. And there was a time we even got food parcels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you were a child. And all of you were children. How did you feel emotionally? For instance, did you cry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't cry. And the crying altogether ceased when we knew exactly that our parents-- after we went to the mannerlager, and we know this night, the next night. There was two nights. I think it was around the 12th of July '44-- went to the gas chambers. At that time, something psychologically happened to us. We suddenly hardened. I mean, we were the same between us. We were the best friends. We could share the last ration of bread, or we could even sell our ration of bread for an aspirin to save one of our friends. But we were more or less indifferent to what's happening around us. And we couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So tears stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way. Well, if you were beaten up savagely by the German sometimes-- I also was once beaten up-- punished by-- I don't remember exactly-- 20 or 30

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with-- with a stick. Maybe I cried even more than actually I should, just to [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But we didn't cry anymore. And I remember, only much later, about five years after the war, one of my beloved teachers died, when I was a student at the academy.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the funeral. And I cried. And I said, oh, thanks God, I'm again human. I can cry. I also remember the very first funeral after the war. At once after the war, it could be a couple of weeks after I return.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was, I think, in Mahrisch Ostrau, by chance. I just happened to be there. And I saw the very first funeral after the war, with horses and music.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Christian--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I observed it. And I said, people must be crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a tremendous ceremony for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: One man, and to make such a fuss about him. It was completely unnatural, because just a month ago I saw thousands of people daily burnt in a pit or killed in other ways. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was your reaction.

speaker: interviewee
text: This emotion came only slowly, slowly back to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you children-- this group of children who came from Theresienstadt, and you were 89, if I am not mistaken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: 89 children.

speaker: interviewee
text: 89 Boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boys, yes. You kept together as a group. How was life in that group? How did you keep together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without knowing it. Anyhow, we didn't have anymore a youth leader, we were only--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have any leader who arranged it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have anymore a leader, like in the other block. We tried to encourage each other. We help each other as much as we could. And our boys had different kind of jobs. Some of them got so-called good job to be laufers or co--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's laufer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Laufers, these were little boys who got a special uniform and a kind of sign on their arm. It was written laufer, for the SS or for the blockalteste. alteste. And they had to serve Dr. Mengele or Schwarzhuber-- the head of Birkenau compound, the SS Obersturmfuhrer Schwarzhuber. And some of our boys got this kind of job in different camps, like the Gypsy camp, the Zigeunerlager, which was next to us. At that time we were in the D compound, and the Gypsy camp was in the E compound. And later I will tell you a story how these boys even helped me when I was in very great troubles.

speaker: interviewer
text: These laufers.

speaker: interviewee
text: These laufers.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a good job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course it was a very good job, because they had enough food. They were very well informed. Some of our boys worked in the so-called post barrack. There was one barrack which was designed for post parcels. The Polish and non-Jews prisoners got, or could get, some parcels. And after the German took the best out of it, they got something of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a kind of barrack only for the post. And one of our boys-- apropos, he is today a professor in Venezuela, for Mathematics-- he worked there. And other boys got appointments, or how you would call it, to help blockalteste.

speaker: interviewee
text: Blockalteste was the head of each block had a blockalteste and a schreiber-- the man who was responsible for all the administration. I remember in the Strafkommando there was a Polish, elderly man-- in our eyes, elder. I mean, he surely was about 35, 40. [INAUDIBLE]-- Jan [INAUDIBLE]. And there was a stubendienst, who was responsible that all our beds and so on should be in order. That was a Polish young man, [? Marian-- ?] [? Marianek, ?] we called him-- and so on. But the blockalteste usually had the help of one of these laufers. And the other boys had different jobs, but mainly our group got the rollwagenkommando, which meant to go with the car. And instead of the horses, there were 20 boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now let's go back to the relations between and the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually, we choose to be together with boys who knew each other from before-- from Theresienstadt-- even from Birkenau--

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you help one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. We helped as much as we could. And a little help-- like, to have good shoes. One of our boys, [INAUDIBLE], who is today in London, an engineer, he was a kind of laufer in the block where they had all the clothes and shoes. And I remember, before we left Auschwitz, it was most important to have a good pair of shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the march.

speaker: interviewee
text: But because, in the marches, the death marches, if you had shoes-- usually, most of the prisoners didn't have shoes at all. They had some wooden clogs, which they're bound together with a string, and so on. And this was fatal, if you had to march--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you got shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --day and night. But he could give us good shoes. And also Kanada, which I mentioned maybe, before-- these people who worked with the newcomers--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the platform.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the platform. And they provided us with some good clothes and shoes. And also, when we went with the rollwagen into a special compound, there were huge and [? non-huge ?] magazines which was sorted out. The very best clothes and all the belongings went to Germany. But the second, third, and other clothes came somehow into camps. And we could get from the women's usually worked there some good clothes which, in the winter of Auschwitz, saved us, in a way, our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So you helped each other, and sort of-- you were sort of a--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to make commune, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --group which was really like a community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually people who slept on the same koje we called it. On the same--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you didn't call it [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. We called it koje.

speaker: interviewer
text: Koje.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were six, seven-- depends on how overpopulated this place was. But after a time, slowly the whole Birkenau, and also Auschwitz, went through an evacuation. They emptied it. And we went to another children block-- block number 29.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Just a second. I want to ask you something else. Now, when you lived together, you had special sort of laws-- things that are allowed to be done and things that are not allowed to be done. You spoke about it in our preview-- that, for instance, to steal from a German is OK, but to steal from other people is not OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had instinctively a law of moral. And that was that between us there is no stealing. And just the opposite, we help as much as we could. Even we helped without that somebody told it to us. We helped to the children from the ghetto Lodz, I think, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, what happened with these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember Lodz was liquidated in the-- in the late summer, it could be, August, September, something like that, maybe October-- '44, of course. And I saw all this transport. I even heard the stories from the Sonderkommando-- how they killed the head of the Lodz-- Rumkowski. He was the so-called King of-- of Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: The head of the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the Judenrat. And we saw these children. They were in a terrible-- I mean, the children who came into the camp-- again, they came in the Zigeunerlager, in the Gypsy camp-- which, by that time, was not any more a Gypsy camp. It had only the name. Because all the Gypsies were killed. They were gassed in the beginning of August-- about the 4th of August, '44. And, later on, only few Germans-- the so-called [? Reise ?] Deutschen--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gypsies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gypsies who had German-- came from German origin, they came, few of them, in our camp, even to the mannerlager, I remember, and later on some of them I met even in Mauthausen. But all the Gypsies, with the children and mothers, all were gassed about around the 4th of August, '44. And later on came the other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like from Lodz. And I remember we helped these children. We collected, between us, soup. And through the electric barbed wire, we were very clever and very, somehow-- we had-- we were very alert.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had good instincts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we pushed through the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbed wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbed wire, which was very dangerous-- the soup for the children. Later on came transport, when they tried to send off all the people from Theresienstadt-- our friend who were still there. And the one who came through the selection came into the camps. We tried to encourage them, and we told them, look, we are so small and weak, but we survived a year. And you can also survive. And we knew what is going to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we told them, try to get out, as quick as possible, to a transport for work. Because at that time, the German needed just hands for repair and workers. So there were selection, and the good, meaning-- I'm meaning, they sent the people out for work. And we helped them as much as we could.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one friend from my town, from Ostrava, asked me if I have a prayer book and some coffee. Of course, a prayer book I couldn't give him, because it wasn't available at the time. But I could manage to bring him some coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, why? Because some of our children worked near the kitchen. And altogether, if a prisoner like us was there over a half a year, and we were nearly a year there, so we knew how to-- we called it organize. And we had it a little better than the rest. But you had the strength of being together-- of this group.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew we had to keep together, instinctively. And so we kept together, as much as we could. Later on, there were selections--

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside your group.

speaker: interviewee
text: --inside our children compound. I remember, it was around Yom Kippur. It has to be October. And once we went from the crematorium to the woman compound, to Auschwitz, with the rollwagen. And I got some letter-- which I didn't know what it's written in. But the women-- the Jewish women worked in a factory of ammunition. And we know now that they provided the people in the crematorium with some ammunition. And later--

speaker: interviewer
text: You are speaking about the people who worked in the Sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the women in--

speaker: interviewer
text: The women in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: One who worked in--

speaker: interviewer
text: They provided them with arms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they tried to provide--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or ammunition.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we also played a little role in it. Meaning, we were couriers with letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were bringing letters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now they were preparing the uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The uprising, actually, I saw-- I mean what I saw-- I remember it was in the beginning of October '44. Suddenly, I've been outside with the rollwagen, when it was in, I think, in the early afternoon. And suddenly we saw flames and a lot of sirens-- alarm went off. And many, many German cars and SS drove towards the crematorium. And later on we knew that the crematorium-- what we called number 3-- the one that had two chimneys-- was blown up, and there was the uprising there. But they caught most of the people. Though the men-- the people in the Sonderkommando, they killed few SS men and some non-Jewish kapos who were very cruel. They killed them. But there was a kind of misunderstanding, and the uprising which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was not really successful?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't successful, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to-- it wasn't successful enough. They couldn't do exactly what they wanted. And they had to do it a little earlier and without the knowledge of the other one. Anyhow, they killed most of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a very famous story, and you were a witness to it, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I saw it when it happened. From far, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, before that, before that uprising--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, what happened before-- that we took letters. And I remember, once I went with this rollwagen, with this letter. And on the way outside, or in between Birkenau and Auschwitz, SS men came, and we had to take off all our clothes. He asked if we had something illegal. Of course, we said no. But then he-- we had to take off all of our clothes. And this letter, which was hidden behind the belt, fell out. And I couldn't-- he saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The SS man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- the SS man saw it. He took the letter, and he took my number. I wasn't punished on the spot at all. He didn't hit me at all. He just took my number. And that was the most frightening and serious thing, because we knew if SS man takes somebody's number, next Sunday-- which was a nonworking day-- I will be called. And so it really happened. I was called to the Gestapo in Birkenau, which happened to be a block in the beginning of the former Gypsy camp. And I had to go there. And I was waiting for hours and hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you went there? Where you afraid?

words.text: How did you feel when you went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I was very, very afraid. But I had enough time to tell the people in the sonderkommando what happened. And they told me what I should say and what I should do. And they contacted this woman-- because her name was mentioned [INAUDIBLE] in the letter-- how to behave and what to say. I had to say that, well, we were just sweeping and cleaning the compound, and I found the letter. And that should have been my excuse, having a letter on me, which was most dangerous. And what actually happened, I was sitting hours. But my friends, who were in this laufers'-- in this compound-- quieted me and tell me, don't be afraid. Everything will be fine. But I was staying on, sitting outside. And I saw a lot of Germans and a great movement of prisoners-- more or less in the mannerlager, I mean, from where I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something unusual--

speaker: interviewee
text: Happened. A lot of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were in the Gestapo, you saw things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I could see from far, because what's happening. And after a couple of hours, the SS man came, and I saw the table with all kinds of instruments for torture, which wasn't so pleasant. He didn't-- I didn't-- I wasn't beaten. I wasn't tortured.

speaker: interviewer
text: Punished

speaker: interviewee
text: Or punished at all. He only asked me a few questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said what I had to say before. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: What, like, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I found it, and I didn't know. And he left me. But when I came back to our camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: He let you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he let me go. Suddenly, more or less half of our boys were missing. There was a tremendous selection. And again, it shows you the irrational thing of Auschwitz, because this horrible, frightened scene saved me. Because if I would be there--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were at the Gestapo, waiting there. And during that time--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a selection in your block with your group. So how many children were--

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less half were left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ja. And they came to some other camps, and somewhere--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were left from your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess about 40. And that is only one illustration how you should never--

speaker: interviewer
text: --good friends of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE], yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember some of them. One was [? Moishe ?] [? Dominic. ?] He had here his brother, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't cry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at that time we didn't cry at all. We lost the crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were only thankful that-- what-- that we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember also, on Yom Kippur, some people didn't eat and kept the soup-- which was very dangerous, because it's very hot and you should always keep your little thing where you kept your soup clean, and so on. And we put the soup under our bed and hoped it will be all right. It was a bit sour in the evening, but somehow we succeeded. But we saw in another block people parade-- few of the Jews parade on Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: We should have a stop now, and we shall soon go on.
